coredata.dc:title: Design and fabrication of a novel platform for detection of 5-fluorouracil as the anti-cancer drug using COFs/MOFs nanocomposite with graphene quantum dots

coredata.dc:creator: Habibi, Biuck
Pashazadeh, Sara
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel modified electrode based on the covalent organic frameworks (COFs) Schiff base network-1 (SNW-1) hybridized with copper-based metal–organic frameworks (Cu-MOFs) embedded by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposite at the glassy carbon electrode, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, was reported. The purpose of this modification is to enhance the detection capabilities of the constructed modified electrode for the 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU), an anti-cancer drug, by increasing its electrocatalytic activity towards the oxidation of 5-FU molecules. The SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite was effectively characterized by the various physicochemical analyses techniques including field emission scanning electron microscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, tunneling electron microscopy, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), X-ray powder diffraction and electrochemical methods. Electrochemical studies showed that the modified electrode, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, has better electrocatalytic performance for oxidizing of the 5-FU compared to the unmodified GCE, GQDs/GCE, SNW-1/GCE, Cu-MOFs/GCE and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GCE. This enhancement can be attributed to large surface area, good electrical conductivity, enhanced electron transport capability of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs and also synergistic effect of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs with GQDs. In optimized conditions, the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE sensor demonstrates excellent properties including a wide linear response ranges (0.01–32 and 32–65 μM), low detection limit (35 nM), high sensitivity (3.8 μAμM-1cm2), long-term signal stability, and reproducibility. It is worth noting that this sensor can accurately determine 5-FU in biological sample, showcasing its potential for practical analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of synthesized materials: (A) GQDs, [B (low magnification), C (high magnification)] SNW-1, (D) Cu-MOFs, (E) SNW-1/Cu-MOFs and (F) SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs. TEM images of (G) GQDs, (H) SNW-1 and (I) SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite.

(A) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms for SNW-1 and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs, and (B) pore-size distributions for SNW-1 and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs and (C) CVs of the GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b), Cu-MOFs/GCE (c), SNW-1/GCE (d), SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GCE (e) and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE (f) in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−.

CVs of the 5-FU at different electrodes: GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b), Cu-MOFs/GCE (c), SNW-1/GCE (d), SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GCE (d), and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE (f). Cyclic voltammetric conditions: scan rate of 100 mVs−1 for 30 µM of 5-FU in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.0).

DPVs of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE in the presence of 0.01–32 and 32–65 μM 5-FU in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.0). Inset is the related linear relationship between the Ip and 5-FU concentrations (calibration curve) in the range of 0.01–32 and 32–65 μM.
(A) DPV curves and (B) bar graph of the interference test of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.0) containing 25 μM 5-FU and other interferons as indicated.
Comparison of analytical parameters of the 5-FU determination at the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE with other modified electrodes.
Results of recovery test of 5-FU in real human serum sample (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Design and fabrication of a novel platform for detection of 5-fluorouracil as the anti-cancer drug using COFs/MOFs nanocomposite with graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz 53714-161, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz 53714-161, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel modified electrode based on the covalent organic frameworks (COFs) Schiff base network-1 (SNW-1) hybridized with copper-based metal–organic frameworks (Cu-MOFs) embedded by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposite at the glassy carbon electrode, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, was reported. The purpose of this modification is to enhance the detection capabilities of the constructed modified electrode for the 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU), an anti-cancer drug, by increasing its electrocatalytic activity towards the oxidation of 5-FU molecules. The SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite was effectively characterized by the various physicochemical analyses techniques including field emission scanning electron microscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, tunneling electron microscopy, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), X-ray powder diffraction and electrochemical methods. Electrochemical studies showed that the modified electrode, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, has better electrocatalytic performance for oxidizing of the 5-FU compared to the unmodified GCE, GQDs/GCE, SNW-1/GCE, Cu-MOFs/GCE and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GCE. This enhancement can be attributed to large surface area, good electrical conductivity, enhanced electron transport capability of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs and also synergistic effect of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs with GQDs. In optimized conditions, the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE sensor demonstrates excellent properties including a wide linear response ranges (0.01–32 and 32–65 μM), low detection limit (35 nM), high sensitivity (3.8 μAμM-1cm2), long-term signal stability, and reproducibility. It is worth noting that this sensor can accurately determine 5-FU in biological sample, showcasing its potential for practical analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is identified as one of the prevalent illnesses that impacts a large number of individuals globally. Cancer is a deadly disease that can silently cause the cells to change and grow uncontrollably in the body. Compared to invasive method such as surgery, the use of anti-cancer drugs is used significantly due to its convenience and cost-effectiveness. Today, several active antitumor drugs are widely used in the treatment of cancers [1–3]. 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) has occupied an important place in biomedicine for more than 50 years due to its function in the chemotherapy of cancer of solid tumors [4–6]. 5-FU is a derivative of uracil a fluorine and classified as an antimetabolite. The mechanism of 5-FU action is that it interferes with DNA production and as a result cell division and tumor growth [7]. Since the time of cell division in cancer cells is longer than other cells, inhibition of cell division damages tumor cells more than other cells [8,9]. Therefore, 5-FU is one of the most important drugs in combination with other drugs for the treatment of breast cancer, pancreas, colorectal, esophagus, stomach and lung [10]. Excessive use of 5-FU is harmful to the human body due to many side effects (hair loss, erythematous dermatitis, skin pigmentation, hand-foot syndrome, and temporary cerebellar dyskinesia) [11,12]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a method to quickly identify the risk of drug overdose for a timely life-saving medical intervention. As a result, there is a necessity for the development of highly selective and sensitive analytical techniques for detect of 5-FU in human body fluids such as blood serum/plasma and urine [13]. These methods are crucial for enhancing the effectiveness of chemotherapy and predicting potential adverse reactions resulting from the retention of 5-FU in the human body [14–16]. Recently, many attempts have been made to determine 5-FU in situ using various techniques such as high performance liquid chromatography [17,18], capillary electrophoresis [19,20], liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry [21,22], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [23,24] and fluorescence spectrometry [25]. Despite the prevalence of cross-reactivity posing a significant challenge in utilizing these analytical methods, it persists due to the structural similarities observed in pyrimidine bases present in authentic samples. Within this framework, the electroanalytical technique emerges as a viable alternative method (due to fast, good selectivity, reduced time analysis, the possibility of in-situ measurements, easy and a highly sensitivity) to the previously mentioned analytical methods [26–34]. In the field of electroanalysis chemistry, the advancement of the preparation technique for the modified electrodes and their characteristics plays a pivotal role in attaining optimal electrocatalytic performance [35]. In the design of electrochemical sensors, a variety of materials are employed as surface modifiers (aiming to expedite electrochemical reactions and enhance electron transfer). Recently, the utilization of nanostructures has become prevalent in sensor technology, owing to their exceptional chemical and physical attributes. Nowadays, among the modifiers used so far, the metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have garnered escalating interest in contemporary times across diverse technological and scientific domains due to their capability to control their pore size, configurations, and chemical attributes [36,37]. MOFs and COFs are two distinct classes of porous materials, each with unique properties and structures. MOFs are composed of metal ions or clusters coordinated with organic ligands. They typically feature a three-dimensional porous structure with well-defined pores and high surface areas [38–40]. The properties of MOFs including pore size, surface area, and functionality, can be precisely tuned by selecting different metal ions and organic ligands. MOFs have garnered significant attention for various applications, including gas storage and separation, catalysis, drug delivery, and sensing. COFs are constructed entirely from covalently bonded organic molecules. On the other hand, COFs represent a category of porous materials comprising light elements (H, B, C, N, and O) linked with organic monomers through robust covalent bonds. COFs are characterized by their high stability, well-defined structures, and tunable properties [41–43]. Similar to MOFs, COFs can be tailored for specific applications by selecting appropriate building blocks and synthetic strategies. MOFs and COFs hybrid combine the advantages of both MOFs and COFs to create new materials with enhanced properties and functionalities. These hybrids can be synthesized by incorporating COFs building blocks into MOFs or vice versa, resulting in structures that exhibit synergistic effects [44–46]. MOFs/COFs hybrids offer opportunities for fine-tuning of pore size, surface functionality, and stability, making them promising candidates for a wide range of applications, including gas storage, catalysis, and sensing [47–49].

On the other hand, nanostructured materials, particularly carbon nanomaterials, have garnered significant interest owing to their distinct physical and chemical attributes, resulting in a vast domain of scientific investigation [50,51]. They offer a promising platform for electroanalysis, particularly in the development of modified electrodes, facilitating electrochemical measurements [52,53]. Among the array of carbon materials available, graphene has been extensively deliberated over the past decade. It is a two-dimensional monolayer of carbon atoms organized in a hexagonal lattice with sp2 hybridized carbon, captivating immense interest due to its exceptional properties [54]. Within various research realms, graphene-based nanomaterials, including graphene oxide, graphene nanoribbons, and graphene quantum dots, have garnered significant attention [55,56]. Notably, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been the focus of extensive scrutiny across diverse research fields due to their unique properties and cost-effective production [57]. GQDs represent a class of zero-dimensional materials sharing similarities with graphene and carbon dots, exhibiting exceptional characteristics linked to quantum confinement and edge effects. Owing to their electrochemical resemblance to graphene, GQDs find widespread utility as suitable electrode materials in various applications such as fuel cells, supercapacitors, photovoltaic cells, and electrochemical sensors [58]. Despite their prevalent use in bioanalysis in recent years, GQDs have not been as extensively explored and employed in the analytical domain. Their distinctive and superior attributes enable GQDs to achieve high-performance levels in sensor applications, emphasizing their potential for widespread utilization [59]. Moreover, the amalgamation of GQDs with nanoparticles has emerged as a pivotal area of investigation due to the introduction of novel and/or enhanced functionalities, offering promising prospects for applications in catalysis, optoelectronic materials, and beyond [60,61]. Nevertheless, the unique properties of the MOFs/COFs hybrids, inadequate electrical conductivity of the MOFs/COFs hybrids materials poses a challenge in realizing direct electron transfer at these hybrids modified electrodes. This limitation can be remedied through the incorporation of conductive substances like GQDs into their hybrids. According to the findings reported in scientific publications, nanohybrids of COFs/MOFs/GQDs have the potential to be used as the sensing materials in the electroanalytical chemistry [62]. COFs/MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite modified electrodes have diverse applications in various fields such as electrochemical sensing, energy storage, catalysis, and environmental monitoring [63]. These nanocomposites can be used for the detection of various analytes, including biomolecules, pollutants, and redox-active species, with high sensitivity and selectivity [62,63].

Based on our current knowledge, the utilization of Schiff base network-1 (SNW-1)/Cu metal–organic frameworks (Cu-MOFs)/ graphene quantum dots nanocomposite (SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs) in detection of 5-FU has not been documented, marking the initial exploration of their efficacy in 5-FU analysis. So, this study explored the use of a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite as an innovative electrochemical sensor for detecting of the 5-FU. Firstly, the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite was analyzed using different spectroscopic and electrochemical methods for physicochemical characterization. Then, the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE was utilized toward the electrooxidation of the 5-FU and fabrication of a novel platform for its detection. The resulting electrochemical sensor demonstrated exceptional sensitivity, long-term stability, selectivity, a wide range of detection concentrations, and low detection limit for 5-FU analysis. Ultimately, the present sensor was successfully used in the electrodetermination of the 5-FU in real biological sample.
A brief description of chemicals and reagents, apparatus, and techniques for the synthesis, characterization and electrochemical analysis are provided in the Supporting Information.

In this investigation, GQDs was synthesised by thermal method under microwave irradiation. To elaborate, a quantity of 5 g of citric acid was dissolved in 5 mL of doubled-distilled water, following which the resulting solution underwent microwave irradiation at a temperature of 180 °C (contained within a Teflon vessel, 150 W, and for a duration of 8 min). Subsequently, the resultant product (resembling a viscous brown liquid) was subjected to filtration utilizing a microporous membrane subsequent to cooling to room temperature [64].

With some modifications, SNW-1 (COFs) was synthesized according to the reported method [65]. First, a solution was prepared by dissolving 1.8 g of terephtaldehyde (TA) in 25 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (using a high-speed stirrer) (solution A). Then, another solution was prepared by dissolving 1.13 g of melamine in 30 mL of DMSO on a magnetic stirrer for 2 h (solution B). Then, solution A was added to solution B inside a round-bottom balloon and degassed for 5 min by ultrasonic method. The obtained solution was poured into the autoclave and placed in the oven at a temperature of 180 °C for 10 h. The obtained product (yellow to white) was washed using acetone, diethyl ether and tetrahydrofuran to remove unreacted components from the product. Finally, it was dried at a temperature of 85 °C (24 h).

Based on reported results [66], for the synthesis of Cu-MOFs, hydrothermal method was used. Briefly, first, 0.15 g of glycine and 0.35 g of copper chloride were dissolved in 10 mL of doubled-distilled water in a beaker. The mixture was stirred for 30 min until well combined. Sodium carbonate solution was added to adjust the pH of the solution in 10. The solution was then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon lined autoclave and placed in an oven at 150˚C for three days. Blue rod-shaped crystals with high quality were obtained from the autoclave. The crystals were gathered, washed with doubled-distilled water, and dried.

For the preparation of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs, a mixture containing 0.2 g of synthesised Cu-MOFs + 3.96 mmol of melamine, and 3.73 mmol of terephtaldehyde + 25 mL of DMSO+5 mL of doubled-distilled water was prepared [67]. The obtained mixture was transferred to the autoclave and placed in the furnace under a specific temperature program (180 °C for 12 h). The obtained product was washed with ethanol and dried (room temperature). To prepare the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite, equal amounts of GQDs and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs were dispersed (final concentration = 2 mg/mL). After sonication (2 h), the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite was collected through centrifugal separation, then dried and used for further characterizations and electrochemical studies.

The construction procedure for modification of the GCE is described in our earlier investigation [67] and with specific information in the Supporting Information. Also, the procedure for the preparation of the modified electrode is visually depicted in Scheme S1 in Supporting Information.

The assessment of the efficiency of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE in the electrocatalytic oxidation and determination of 5-FU in human blood serum sample was conducted with a detailed description of the preparation process along with the preparation of real sample in Supporting Information. The determination of 5-FU was carried out using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique and the standard addition method, including three repetitive assays for each sample (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structure analysis of the nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological and compositional characteristics of materials have the significant impact on their physical and chemical properties. The utilization of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis is crucial for capturing potential topographic information. The morphology of synthesized materials: GQDs, SNW-1, Cu-MOFs, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs are characterized by the SEM and the results presented in Fig. 1 . SEM of GQDs (image A), shows sphere-shaped morphology and there are no flake-like structures. Also, the SEM of SNW-1 (image B), shows obviously spherical morphology in the low magnification and homogeneous rough surface, while in higher magnification (image C) the surface morphology of the SNW-1 clearly demonstrates a consistent sheet structure and the surface appears rougher and more aggregated. The morphology of Cu-MOFs (image D), has rod-shaped and needle structure with the average diameter of 100 nm. In the SNW-1 hybridized with Cu-MOFs (image E), the morphology is similar to the Cu-MOFs with a difference that a string of rosary beads is connected to SNW-1 particles with irregular spherical grains that have stretched out into thin strings due to accumulation. This attachment is made possible through the amine and aldehyde groups found in the constituent monomers [47]. In this research, the Cu-MOFs particles were introduced to the SNW-1 synthesis medium to engage using the NH2 group of MOFs linkers. Additionally, in the final material, a specific amount of synthetic nanohybrid was incorporated into the GQDs synthesis process to alter the surface of the nanohybrid, as seen in image F for the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite. As a result, during synthesis, a structure similar to core shell formation is created, where SNW-1/Cu-MOFs forms on the surface of the GQDs; SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs.

The elemental distribution map of the end product, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite is illustrated in Fig. S1 which clearly reveals the presence of Cu, C, N, S, O and Na elements (along with weight percent of the elements) in the sample and represents the successful synthesis of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite without any element impurities in their composition (upper spectrum and its inset). On the other hand, the elemental mapping of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs was drawned which shows the surface distribution of elements of Cu, C, N, S, O, Na and all of them. These findings are illustrated in Fig. S1 (below elemental mapping).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized to investigate comprehensive details regarding the internal structure, morphology and crystalline architecture of the prepared nanocomposite and its components and illustrated in Fig. 1. The TEM image of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1G. The fine agglomerated crystalline patterns of the GQDs can be seen in this image. The image presented in Fig. 1H shows the TEM of the SNW-1 particles with the irregular spherical grains shape. Fig. 1I shows the TEM image of prepared nanocomposite, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs. The visual data unequivocally illustrates the dual presence of the respective species, where Cu-MOFs particles are depicted as tree branches (darker regions), GQDs and SNW-1 particles are represented as snowflakes situated atop the Cu-MOFs (brighter regions).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis is a technique used to analyze the crystal structure of materials. Fig. S2 shows the XRD patterns of the GQDs, SNW-1, Cu-MOFs, and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs. For the GQDs, XRD pattern shows a wide peak centered at around 25° which is related to (002) plane (JCPDS No. 41–1487) of the GQDs [57,58,68]. The XRD pattern of SNW-1 demonstrates the main characteristic and a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 15–30°, suggesting a structure that is amorphous with a low level of crystallinity (exactly in accordance with the reported literatures [41,42,47,65,69–75]). The XRD pattern of Cu-MOFs displays the sharp diffraction peaks indicating predominantly crystalline material, which the diffraction peaks corresponding to (200), (110), (001), (020), (201), (111), (220), (112), (120), (023), (032), (123), (041), (220) and (213) planes can be attributed to the successful synthesis of Cu-MOFs as indicated by previous research [66]. Additionally, there are no conspicuous prominent peaks observed in the XRD profile, suggesting that the produced Cu-MOFs material possesses a relatively high level of purity. For the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite, the tiny peak at ∼ 24.94° related to the carboxyl groups of modified GQDs plane (002) confirming the effective combination of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs and GQDs in the nanocomposite and all diffraction peaks corresponding to the Cu-MOFs.

In the context of the adsorption–desorption process involving N2, the specific surface area (SSA) was derived utilizing the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) methodology. Fig. 2 illustrates the adsorption–desorption process of N2 (Fig. 2A) and the pore distribution profiles of the SNW-1 and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs (Fig. 2B) as analyzed through the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model. The calculated SSA values for SNW-1 and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs were determined to be 286.5 and 413.5 m2/g−1, respectively. The findings indicate that the incorporation of SNW-1 particles within the nanocomposite enhances its surface area, leading to the formation of new micropores characterized by a substantial surface area. Furthermore, these graphical representations facilitate the elucidation of the microporous and mesoporous characteristics of the synthesized material. In the nanocomposite SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs, the Cu-MOFs/GQDs is integrated with SNW-1, thereby augmenting the mesoporous attributes of the resultant composite material, which culminates in an enhancement of both the volume and diameter of its mesopores. Notably, the pore diameters for SNW-1 and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs were quantified to be 3.137 and 3.08 nm, respectively, thereby substantiating their microporous nature.

In order to investigate the electrochemical characteristics of materials, various modified electrodes were constructed: GQDs/GCE, Cu-MOFs/GCE, SNW-1/GCE, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GCE and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE and the cyclic voltammetric method was implied in the solution of KCl (0.1 M) containing [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, as a redox probe. In the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) (Fig. 2C) of the GQDs/GCE, SNW-1/GCE, Cu-MOFs/GCE, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GCE and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, a pair of redox peaks is clearly evident, similar to those identified at the bare GCE. The measured peak currents on the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs /GCE demonstrate higher value in comparison with recorded values on the other modified electrodes. Under identical experimental conditions, the peak currents follow a recognizable sequence of GQDs/GCE<Cu-MOFs/GCE<SNW-1/GCE<SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GCE<SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE. Thus, the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE may possess favorable conductivity and high active surface area. It is important to observe that the peak currents recorded on the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE exhibit the greatest magnitude, suggesting that the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs likely enhances the electron transfer capacity. The active surface area modified electrode was calculated on the basis of the plots [peak current versus (scan rate)0.5] and Randles-Sevick equation;

, via cyclic voltammetric method in the 0.1 M KCl solution containing [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (5 mM). The interpretation of each parameter is elucidated in the literature that has been previously reported [76]. Based on the calculation, the active surface areas were found as 0.061, 0.123, 0.158, 0.196, and 0.256 cm2 for bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, Cu-MOFs/GCE, SNW-1/GCE, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GCE, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, respectively. The result suggested that SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE has more electrochemical active sites and can enhance the electrochemical signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrooxidation of the 5-FU at the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical performances and comparative analysis upon electrocatalysis of the modified electrodes is studied by cyclic voltammetry. The CVs responses of the 5-FU (30 μM) in the potential ranges of 0.6 to 1.0 V vs Ag/AgCl in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH=7.0) with a scan rate of 100 mV/s at the unmodified GCE, GQDs/GCE, Cu-MOFs/GCE, SNW-1/GCE, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GCE, and SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE are recorded and depicted in Fig. 3 . As illustrated in Fig. 3 (curve a), the unmodified GCE shows almost no electrochemical activity toward 5-FU oxidation. While other CV curves have oxidation peak with peak current as 6.41 μA at the GQDs/GCE, 10.7 μA for the Cu-MOFs/GCE, 16.01 μA on the SNW-1/GCE), 24.81 μA at the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GCE, and 46.31 μA for the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE. Also, as can be seen in Fig. 3 (curve f) the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE gives a well-definedand appreciable peak for5-FU oxidation with highest current compared to all step by step modified GCEs mentioned above. The obtained results indicate that the oxidation process of 5-FU on the GCE surface is significantly enhances due to the dispersion of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs, which not only maintains the conductivity of GQDs, but also improves its electrocatalytic ability with enhances active surface area. Also, as explained, this phenomenon (highest electrocatalytic performance of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE) may be attributable to the higher specific surface area of SNW-1 and GQDs and synergistic contributions of SNW-1, Cu-MOFs and GQDs, which leads to excellent electron transfer ability and electrocatalytic activity. On the other hand, an excellent increase in the current response implies that the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE accelerates the electron transfer rate and can be said to act as a stimulus for the electrochemical reaction. Finally, as can be seen in Fig. 3, no reduction peak is observed for 5-FU in the reverse scan, so based on our results and scientific findings, it can be ascribed that the 5-FU oxidation is irreversible on all the modified electrodes [2,7,26–34,67,68,75,77].

To obtain the higher performance of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, experimental conditions such as the amount of modifier (film thickness), pH and scan rate were optimized. The film thickness is a key parameter, likely to affect the voltammetric response of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE in electrooxidation of 5-FU. Loading of the suitable amount of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs modifier can increases the peak current of the constructed modified electrode and improves its electroanalysis sensitivity. As a result, various amounts of modifier were tested during the electrode modification process (0.1 mg/mL to 4 mg/mL). The relationship between Ip and the amount of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs modifier in the electrode modification process was evaluated using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique in the presence of 10 µM 5-FU in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH=7.0) (not shown here). As apperceived, the current signal of 5-FU increases gradually as the amount of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs modifier is increased (from 0.1 to 2 mg/mL) and the highest electrode signal observed at 2 mg/mL of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs. As the amount of modifier is increased even more, it leads to a reduction in the adhesion of the coated layer at the electrode surface and decrease in peak current due to the thicker layer of coating on the electrode surface [78,79]. For more concentrated dispersions (from 2 to 4 mg/mL), the peak current decreased as the film thickness increased, leading to a reduction in mass transfer to the electrode. Actually, the higher amounts of modifier (>2 mg/mL) lead to roughness on the modified electrode surface, which obstructs the electrode and hinders 5-FU from getting through, leading to flattened peaks and decreased modified electrode signal. Therefore, 2 mg/mL was selected as the optimum value for future experiments involving 5-FU electrocatalysis and electroanalysis.

The pH of the supporting electrolyte is a critical factor that can strongly affect the modified electrode response and changes the sensitivity of the fabricated sensor, especially when protons are involved in the electrochemical reaction. In order to determine the appropriate pH for the electrochemical analysis of 5-FU, the pH of the PBS which was used as the detection medium changed between 3 and 10 and the response of the modified electrode was determined by cyclic voltammetry technique (Fig. S3). According to the findings results in inset of Fig. S3, the oxidation peak current (Ip) of 5-FU gradually increases from pH 3.0 to 7.0, and reaches its maximum value at about pH=7.0. Subsequently, the peak current decreased when the pH values increased above 7, suggesting its improved behavior at about pH 7.0. Thus, pH=7.0 was chosen for further analysis. From the chemical point, 5-FU has one pKa values (pKa = 8.1) and the drug is negatively charged at pH higher than 8.1 [80–82]. The decrease of the peak current at pH higher than 7.0 might be a consequence of the 5-FU pKa value (8.1). In the electrochemical measurements that were carried out in pH values higher than 7.0, probably an electrostatic repulsion occurred between the negatively charged 5-FU and the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE surface due to the deformation 5-FU molecules. It should be noted that, the voltammetric response of 5-FU is most pronounced in a neutral environment, consistent with the findings of other researchers [67,68,75,77,80–82]. Also, as it can be seen, with increasing the pH of solution, the peak potential shifts to more negative potentials, indicating that the involvement of protons in the oxidation reaction. Inset of Fig. S3 illustrates the relationship between Ep and pH through a linear regression equation (1).
(1)
E p
V
= 0.0605
p H + 1.6006
R 2
= 0.9952
As shown in the above equation, the gradient of 0.0605 is close to the expected Nernstian value of 0.0592, indicating that the oxidation reaction of 5-FU at the present modified electrode involves the equivalent quantity of electrons and protons [80–82].

To investigate and gather additional details regarding the kinetic mechanism and regulation of the 5-FU electrooxidation process at the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, the impact of varying the potential scan rate on the peak current response was studied using cyclic voltammetry technique in a 0.1 M PBS solution (pH=7.0) containing 30 µM 5-FU and illustrated in Fig. S4 A. As can be seen, the anodic peaks current were increased linearly with the v0.5 (square root of scan rate) and the plot of IP as a function of V0.5 for modified electrode was obtained (Fig. S4 B) with excellent linearity equation of Ip(μA) = 9.7665 + 10.86v0.5, (R2 = 0.9926). This suggests that the electrooxidation process 5-FU at the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE follows a diffusion-controlled process [67,68,75,77,80–82]. Additionally, a direct correlation was found between the logarithm of Ip and the logarithm of v, with a slope of 0.4517 (Fig. S4 C). This slope is closely aligned with the expected theoretical value of 0.5 for a diffusion-controlled process [83]. Moreover, in this study we also examine the correlation between the Ep (anodic peak potential) and v (scan rate). Findings indicate that as the scan rate increases, the peak potential shifts towards more positive values. A graph (not shown here) representing Ep versus log υ was constructed, demonstrating a linear relationship that suggests the modified electrode reaction is irreversible (as seen already in the electrooxidation of 5-FU at reverse scan in subsection 3.2). In the case of an irreversible and diffusion-controlled electrode process, Ep can be determined using the equation derived from Laviron's model [84]:

In equation (2), the parameters n, F, R, T, α, E0, and ks have their standard meanings [n = number of electrons transferred; F=Faraday constant (96,485.3C eq−1); R=universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1K−1); T=absolute temperature (298 K); α = transfer coefficient; E0 = formal redox potential; and ks = catalytic rate constant]. The values for αn and ks can be determined by calculating the slope and intercept of Eq. (2). According to the equation mentioned above, the relationship between the Ep and the log v is linear with following relationship:
(3)

Based on the slope of the plot, αn (α = transfer coefficient; n: number of electrons transferred) was determined to be 1.23. The intercept of mentioned plot was utilized in order to determine the value of rate constant (ks). By using the Laviron equation, the ks was obtained as 1.87 s−1. Based on the obtained results and according to the literature reported for 5-FU electrooxidation [3,26–34,77,85], the electrooxidation mechanism of 5-FU can be expressed as in Scheme S2 at Supporting Information, in which the two electrons and two protons were participated in the oxidation reaction.

Considering that the sensor's resistance to interfering substances (which can affect the sensor's output) is crucial in identifying the drug accurately using the sensor in real-time applications. Therefore, it is very important to investigate the effects of interfering species in the electrodetermination of 5-FU. In interference studies, it is usually tried to select species that are present in real samples and have the highest similarity to the analyte. Therefore, anti-interference studies of the constructed sensor; SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, was carried out for the selective detection of 25 µM 5-FU simultaneously with 10-fold excess concentration interfering species in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH=7.0) [including ascorbic acid (AA), carbofuran (CF), dopamine (DA), ibuprofen (IBU), naproxen (NPX), theophylline (THE), catechol (CC), and uric acid (UA)] (Fig. 5 A). The obtained current response (less than 5 %) showed the high selectivity of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE in detection of 5-FU (Fig. 5B), indicating that the proposed modified electrode has high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the reusability, reproducibility, stability and repeatability of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Reusability, reproducibility, stability and repeatability were crucial characteristics for evaluating the effectiveness of the modified electrodes or sensors. The sensor's reusability was tested by using the one modified electrode for multiple DPV detections. A single modified electrode was immersed in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.0) containing 25 μM 5-FU and DPV was recorded (not shown here). After being rinsed in doubled-distilled water for 1.0 min, the sensor was reintroduced into the 5-FU solution. This procedure was repeated 20 times, and the RSD value of 3.21 % was observed which indicates that the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE has excellent reusability for detecting of 5-FU.

A quick assessment of the reproducibility study was conducted using various modified electrodes in the optimum experimental settings. Hence, in order to investigates the reproducibility experiment, seven identical modified electrodes were made and utilized to measure the 5-FU (25 μM) using the DPV method (not shown here). The calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.71 % for the response of seven the modified electrodes shows that the preparation process has excellent reproducibility. The stability of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE sensor was carefully examined through a detailed evaluation. A rigorous stability assessment was carried out by testing the constructed sensor to daily analyses 28 days by DPV technique (in 0.1 M PBS pH=7.0 containing 10 µM 5-FU), recording its response to 5-FU (not shown here). Following the assessments, the calculated %RSD of the recorded answers, which was determined to be 3.1 %, confirms the higher strong stability of the sensor over an extended duration. This distinguishes the ability of the sensor to provide stable and reliable results in long-term analyses. Finally, the present modified electrode was tested for its ability to produce consistent results when exposed to the anti-cancer drug, 5-FU. The modified electrode was employed for ten repeatability studies. During each repeatability test, the modified electrode displayed similar results to the original DPV peaks (not shown here). The slight decline in measurements could be attributed to the saturation of the modified electrode compared to its initial value. The %RSD value was determined to be 2.53 %. The lower %RSD suggested that the sensing system exhibited excellent repeatability for the 5-FU. Based on the obtained results, can conclude that the improved modified electrode exhibits high reusability, reproducibility, stability and repeatability. These characteristics support further exploration of the sensor in actual sample tests.

To assess the usability and practicality of the constructed sensor, it is important to test it in the real sample’s analysis. The constructed sensor; SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, was used to detect the 5-FU in human serum sample to test its effectiveness for real analysis. The human serum samples were diluted with 0.1 M PBS at pH=7.0 and then analyzed using by the present sensor in the optimal conditions. The reliability of the sensor was assessed through the standard addition method and DPV technique, where the specific amounts of standard 5-FU solution were added to the human samples. The obtained recovery range values of 5-FU in human serum samples (98.20–100.20 %) with low %RSDs (2.42 %), are acceptable and the obtained results were listed in Table 2 . As a result, it is evident that the suggested sensor; SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, is a suitable choice for identifying the 5-FU in real samples with a substantial recovery range.

In this study, SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs nanocomposite was synthesized using a straightforward method, and then utilized for the construction of an electrochemical sensor for the quick, accurate and selective determination of 5-FU. The physicochemical properties of synthesized nanocomposite were characterized by different techniques (SEM, EDX, TEM, BET, XRD and electrochemical). The cyclic voltammetry analysis of 5-FU showed that the use of SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE enhanced the electrocatalytic performance in the electrooxidation of 5-FU and suggested the designed modified electrode demonstrated superior ability in oxidation of 5-FU in comparison to the unmodified and other step by step modified electrodes. Taking advantage of the fast electron transfer capability, large surface area, and excellent electrocatalytic abilities of the SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs, the sensing platform that was constructed has shown impressive performance when it comes to electrochemical detection of 5-FU. So that, the constructed sensor shows a wide linear range (0.01–32 and 32–65 μM) and low detection limit (35 nM), as well as other characteristics such as good reusability, high stability, significant reproducibility and exceptional selectivity. The present sensor; SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, has superior practical applications because it showed the excellent results for analyzing of the 5-FU levels in human serum sample. Consequently, because of the superior conductivity, strong electron transfer capacity and high stability of the prepared nanocomposite; SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs, the proposed and constructed sensor; SNW-1/Cu-MOFs/GQDs/GCE, has the potential to be a strong competitor for the development of innovative sensor system for a diverse range of electrochemical applications and pharmaceutical analysis.
Biuck Habibi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Sara Pashazadeh: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis.
